name: Nommo
description: The Nommo are primordial ancestral spirits (sometimes referred to as deities) venerated by the Dogon people of Mali. They are amphibious beings, often depicted as hermaphroditic or androgynous, with fish-like bodies, smooth skin, and jointed limbs. They are considered the first living beings created by Amma, the supreme creator god. According to Dogon cosmology, the Nommo descended from the sky in an ark called the 'Andoumboulou' after Amma sacrificed one of their twins to bring order to the universe. Their blood, spilled during the sacrifice, turned the earth red and created the stars. The Nommo are responsible for teaching humanity agriculture, weaving, and the secrets of the universe. They also brought language and social structure. They are associated with water, fertility, and the renewal of life. The Nommo are believed to reside in bodies of water and are often invoked in rituals related to rain and harvest. They play a crucial role in the Dogon's understanding of creation, death, and rebirth, serving as intermediaries between Amma and humanity. Their knowledge is passed down through generations through intricate myths and rituals. The Nommo are also linked to the Sirius star system, a connection that has fascinated researchers and sparked debate about the origins of Dogon astronomical knowledge.
subtitle: Primordial ancestral spirits of the Dogon

keyMyths.title: The Descent of the Nommo
keyMyths.description: The Nommo descended from the sky in an ark, bringing civilization to humanity. They taught the Dogon people agriculture and weaving.

keyMyths.title: The Sacrifice of the Nommo Twin
keyMyths.description: One of the Nommo twins was sacrificed by Amma to bring order to the universe. The blood from the sacrifice colored the Earth red.

keyMyths.title: The Nommo and Sirius
keyMyths.description: The Nommo are associated with the Sirius star system, holding knowledge about its companion star, Sirius B. This knowledge is central to Dogon cosmology.
keyMyths.source: Temple, Robert K. G. The Sirius Mystery.

sources.author: Griaule, Marcel

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, kola nuts, and other symbolic items are made to the Nommo at shrines and during ceremonies to honor them and seek their blessings.

worshipPractices.name: Sacrifices
worshipPractices.description: Animal sacrifices, particularly of goats and chickens, are sometimes performed to appease the Nommo and ensure their continued favor. The blood is believed to nourish the spirits.

festivals.name: Sigui Festival
festivals.description: The Sigui festival is a major Dogon celebration that honors the ancestors, including the Nommo. It involves elaborate rituals, dances, and the creation of a new Sigui mask, symbolizing the renewal of life and the continuation of Dogon traditions. It is a complex ceremony linked to the Sirius star system.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Nommo have gained international recognition through anthropological studies and popular culture, influencing artistic and spiritual movements. They are often cited in discussions of ancient astronaut theories and alternative interpretations of human origins, though these interpretations are not part of traditional Dogon beliefs.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: According to Dogon cosmology, Amma, the supreme creator god, initially created a flawed, single Nommo. Dissatisfied, Amma sacrificed this first Nommo and scattered its body parts throughout the cosmos, purifying the universe. From these scattered remains, twin pairs of Nommo were born, representing perfect androgynous beings. These Nommo descended from the sky in an ark, bringing with them the seeds of all life and the knowledge of civilization. The ark's landing is a central event in Dogon mythology, marking the beginning of human society and the transmission of divine wisdom. The Nommo are thus considered both ancestral spirits and the progenitors of humanity, embodying the essential duality of male and female principles.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One of the most prominent myths surrounding the Nommo involves their sacrifice and resurrection. After descending to Earth, the Nommo sacrificed one of their own to purify the land and ensure the fertility of the crops. This act of self-sacrifice is seen as a foundational event in Dogon cosmology, mirroring the initial sacrifice of the flawed Nommo by Amma. Another significant myth recounts the Nommo's role in teaching the Dogon people the arts of agriculture, weaving, and metalworking. They are also credited with establishing the complex social and religious structures that govern Dogon society. Stories of the Nommo often emphasize their connection to water, highlighting their amphibious nature and their ability to control the elements. They are seen as benevolent beings who guide and protect humanity, but also as powerful forces to be respected and revered.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: The worship of the Nommo is deeply embedded in Dogon religious practices. Shrines are dedicated to them throughout Dogon villages, often located near water sources. Priests and elders play a crucial role in maintaining these shrines and conducting ceremonies to honor the Nommo. Offerings of food, kola nuts, and other symbolic items are made to the Nommo to seek their blessings and ensure the prosperity of the community. The Dogon also perform elaborate dances and rituals that reenact the myths of the Nommo, celebrating their role in creation and their ongoing influence on human affairs. The Sigui festival, held every sixty years, is a particularly important event dedicated to the ancestors, including the Nommo. The festival involves the creation of a new Sigui mask, which symbolizes the renewal of life and the perpetuation of Dogon traditions. The Nommo are also invoked in healing ceremonies and divination practices, as they are believed to possess the power to cure illnesses and reveal the future.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Nommo have had a lasting impact on Dogon culture and continue to be a central element of their identity. Their image and symbolism appear in Dogon art, architecture, and religious practices. The intricate cosmological knowledge associated with the Nommo, particularly their connection to the Sirius star system, has also attracted considerable attention from scholars and researchers. Outside of Dogon culture, the Nommo have gained recognition through anthropological studies and popular culture. They are often cited in discussions of ancient astronaut theories and alternative interpretations of human origins, though these interpretations are not part of traditional Dogon beliefs. The Nommo serve as a reminder of the rich and complex mythology of the Dogon people and their profound understanding of the universe. Their legacy extends beyond their religious significance, influencing artistic and spiritual movements and sparking ongoing debates about the origins of human knowledge and civilization.

shortDescription: Amphibious ancestral spirits in Dogon mythology, the Nommo are primordial beings descended from the sky, bringing civilization and knowledge to humanity.
